note: The ellipsis char … (U+2026) is a sentence terminator for capitalization.
Leading uppercase avoids first-letter-cap noise; isolates the space-collapse rule.
The (?!\d) lookahead protects number groups like 3,14 from getting a space.
Decimal 2.5 is shielded so its dot is not a sentence boundary.
A full URL survives post-process intact (no punctuation/cap mangling inside).
A trailing '.' is split off the URL so it still ends the sentence, URL stays intact.

mailto: has no // authority so the URL rule misses it; it must be shielded before the EMAIL pass so the whole href survives instead of a bare 'mailto:' getting a space injected after its colon (spintax-js#41).
The production bug: the email shield carved contact@example.com out of the href, leaving 'mailto:' whose colon then got a space, producing a malformed 'mailto: contact@…' link.
A trailing '.' is split off the mailto: URI (like URLs) so it still ends the sentence and caps the next word, address intact.
tel: URI shielded before the 'space after colon' rule can split 'tel:' from its number.
Domain is shielded; the '.' after it still ends the sentence and caps the next word.
Single-token abbreviation 'соц.' is whitelisted, so 'сети' is NOT falsely capitalized.
'Mr.' is whitelisted, so 'smith' is not treated as a new sentence.

Multi-dot abbreviation 'e.g.' (2+ dotted groups) is shielded by step 5a. NB: step 5a uses \b, which — like PHP PCRE without UCP — is ASCII, so it shields ASCII abbreviations but NOT a Cyrillic one like т.д. (parity holds; the ru whitelist covers single-token Cyrillic via a \p{L} lookbehind).
Leading/trailing whitespace collapses then trims; first letter capitalizes.

Spanish opens a question with an inverted mark. The capitalizer upper-cases the first CHARACTER after a sentence boundary; '¿' has no uppercase form, so without an opener rule the real first letter stays lowercase. SENTENCE_OPENERS = '¿¡' only — quotes/brackets are NOT openers (they also close, and capitalizing after them would mangle list markers).
Opener sits between the sentence-ending punctuation and the first letter. Covers both inverted marks.

An opener binds to the word it opens: '¿ qué' -> '¿qué'. This spacing pass MUST run before capitalization, so the capitalizer sees the real first letter and not a space. The second input ('¿cómo', no space) proves the rule is not merely a whitespace artefact.
The plugin's primary output is HTML paragraphs, so an opener after a block-level tag is the commonest real case of all.
Multi-line templates hit the newline capitalization rule rather than the sentence-punctuation one.

Guards the deliberate NARROWNESS of SENTENCE_OPENERS. A quote or bracket is not an opener: capitalizing after one would break list markers like '(a) primero'. If this case ever starts capitalizing, the contract was widened by accident.

Cross-engine guard for the NARROWNESS of SENTENCE_OPENERS: a quote is not an opener. Both straight (") and guillemet («) forms stay lowercase after a sentence boundary, while the start-of-text capitalisation still fires — so the rule is proven to be narrow rather than simply dead. A TS unit test alone would not bind the PHP engine; this is the parity gate. Companion to postprocess/opener-set-excludes-quotes-and-brackets, which guards the bracket case.

RAE's form for a sentence that is both a question and an exclamation opens with TWO marks. A lead that allows exactly one opener leaves the most Spanish construction there is uncapitalized — and the closing '?!' is a run, so it must survive the spacing pass intact too. Both halves of the defect are visible in this one case.
The other order RAE sanctions. Locks that the lead is a RUN of openers rather than a hardcoded '¡¿' pair.

The opened word is routinely wrapped in markup, which puts a tag AFTER the opener. The lead has to allow tags on BOTH sides of it: 'boundary → tags → opener → letter' was handled, 'boundary → opener → tags → letter' was not.
The commonest real shape of all: an HTML paragraph whose Spanish question opens with a link. Exercises the block-tag capitalizer through an opener followed by an anchor.

A run of sentence punctuation is ONE sentence end, not several. The 'space after .!?' rule fired between the marks and shredded the copy in EVERY language: 'Wait... what' became 'Wait. . . what'. The Unicode '…' was never in the [.!?] class and so was never affected — which is exactly why the corpus's ellipsis fixture missed this.
A run that ends the text gets no trailing space. Guards the backtracking trap: a greedy '+' alone still backs INTO the run to satisfy the lookaheads, yielding 'Wow!! !'.
'?!' is one sentence end: it must not be split, and the capitalizer must still fire on the word after it.
The space belongs AFTER the whole run, never inside it — so a word glued to the run is separated from it, once.
